title: Honeycomb toffee
subtitle: British puffed caramel sweet made with bicarbonate of soda, also called cinder toffee
excerpt: Golden, airy, crunchy caramel made by cooking sugar and golden syrup to the hard crack stage then adding bicarbonate of soda, which causes an immediate dramatic foam that sets to a porous, honeycomb-textured slab. Also called cinder toffee, sponge toffee, or hokey pokey. The whole process takes under 10 minutes once the syrup is cooking. Optional dark chocolate coating turns it into a version of a chocolate honeycomb bar.
sourceNotes: Honeycomb toffee (cinder toffee, sponge toffee) is a British and Commonwealth confection documented in household cookery literature from the nineteenth century. Mrs Beeton's Book of Household Management (various editions, Project Gutenberg) includes hard sugar candy preparations, and the principle of using bicarbonate of soda to aerate hot caramel is described in British confectionery books of the Victorian period. The resulting honeycomb texture is consistent across British and Scottish home candy-making literature.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Store in an airtight tin with baking paper between layers for up to 2 weeks. Do not refrigerate: condensation will dissolve the honeycomb. Keep away from humidity.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: target sugar temperature: 150°C (hard crack stage). The mixture should be deep amber before the bicarbonate of soda is added. Do not go above 155°C or the caramel will be bitter. Have the bicarbonate measured and the tin lined before the syrup reaches temperature.

recipeTools.notes: large enough to hold the foaming mixture; it will triple in volume
lined with baking paper and lightly greased

glossaryTerms.definition: When bicarbonate of soda is stirred into hot caramel at the hard crack stage, it reacts with the acidic sugar to release carbon dioxide gas. The gas cannot escape from the viscous caramel and forms thousands of small bubbles that expand the mixture to three or four times its original volume in seconds. The mixture must be poured immediately as the caramel sets quickly and the foam structure collapses if disturbed. Use a large pan: the foam rises dramatically and a saucepan that seems oversized before adding the soda will feel just right when it foams. Measure the bicarbonate accurately: too little gives a dense, hard candy; too much gives a bitter flavour.

content.text: Everything must be ready before the soda goes in: the tin lined, the thermometer clipped, and no distractions.
The bicarbonate reaction is immediate and dramatic
: stir once, pour at once. The caramel sets in under a minute.

attrs.prepNote: measured and ready before cooking starts

content.text: Line a baking tray with baking paper and lightly grease the paper. Measure the bicarbonate of soda into a small dish and keep it next to the hob. Combine the sugar, golden syrup, and 2 tablespoons of water in a large saucepan. Stir over medium heat until the sugar dissolves, then stop stirring. Clip on the sugar thermometer.

Cook over medium-high heat without stirring until the mixture reaches 150°C and is deep amber. Remove from the heat immediately. Add the bicarbonate of soda and stir just once or twice with a long wooden spoon: the mixture will foam up dramatically. Immediately pour onto the prepared tray without spreading or pressing; it will level itself. Leave undisturbed for at least 30 minutes until completely cold and set.

Break into shards or pieces. To coat in chocolate: melt the dark chocolate in a bowl over barely simmering water. Dip each piece halfway into the chocolate and set on baking paper to firm. Store in an airtight tin, not the fridge.
